## DEEPENING HIDDEN REPRESENTATIONS FROM - PRE TRAINED LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Transformer-based pre-trained language models have proven to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. However, current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder’s final layer when fine-tuning the downstream tasks. We argue that only taking single layer’s output restricts the power of pre-trained representation. Thus we deepen the representation learned by the model by fusing the hidden representation in terms of an explicit HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), which automatically absorbs the complementary representation with respect to the output from the final layer. Utilizing RoBERTa as the backbone encoder, our proposed improvement over the pre-trained models is shown effective on multiple natural language understanding tasks and help our model rival with the state-of-the-art models on the GLUE benchmark.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Language representation is essential to the understanding of text. Recently, pre-trained language models based on Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) such as GPT (Radford et al., 2018), BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) have been shown to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. These models have since continued to achieve new state-of-the-art results on a variety of natural language processing tasks. They include question answering (Rajpurkar et al., 2018; Lai et al., 2017), natural language inference (Williams et al., 2018; Bowman et al., 2015), named entity recognition (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003), sentiment analysis (Socher et al., 2013) and semantic textual similarity (Cer et al., 2017; Dolan & Brockett, 2005).

Normally, Transformer-based models are pre-trained on large-scale unlabeled corpus in an unsupervised manner, and then fine-tuned on the downstream tasks through introducing task-specific output layer. When fine-tuning on the supervised downstream tasks, the models pass directly the output of Transformer encoder’s final layer, which is considered as the contextualized representation of input text, to the task-specific layer.

However, due to the numerous layers (i.e., Transformer blocks) and considerable depth of these pretrained models, we argue that the output of the last layer may not always be the best representation of the input text during the fine-tuning for downstream tasks. Devlin et al. (2019) shows diverse combinations of different layers’ outputs of the pre-trained BERT result in distinct performance on CoNLL-2003 Named Entity Recognition (NER) task (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003). Peters et al. (2018b) points out for pre-trained language models, including Transformer, the most transferable contextualized representations of input text tend to occur in the middle layers, while the top layers specialize for language modeling. Therefore, the onefold use of the last layer’s output may restrict the power of the pre-trained representation.

In this paper, we propose an extra network component design for Transformer-based model, which is capable of adaptively leveraging the hidden information in the Transformer’s hidden layers to refine the language representation. Our introduced additional components include two main additional components:
1. HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

2. Fusion network integrates the hidden information extracted by the HIRE with Transformer final layer’s output through two steps of functionalities, leading to a refined contextualized language representation.

Taking advantage of the robustness of RoBERTa by using it as our backbone Transformer-based encoder (Liu et al., 2019c), we conduct experiments on GLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2018), which consists of nine Natural Language Understanding (NLU) tasks. With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset. Keeping the backbone Transformer model unchanged on its architecture, pre-training procedure and training objectives, we get comparable performance with other state-of-the-art models on the GLUE leaderboard, which verifies the effectiveness of the proposed HIRE enhancement over Transformer model.

## 2.1 TRANSFORMER-BASED ENCODER

Transformer-based encoder is responsible for encoding input text into contextualized representation. Let _{w_ 1 _, . . . , wn}_ represent a sequence of _n_ words of input text, Transformer-based encoder encodes the input sequence into its universal contextualized representation _**R** ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ :

where _d_ is the hidden size of the encoder and _**R**_ is the output of Transformer-based encoder’s last layer which has the same length as the input text. We call it **preliminary representation** in this paper to distinguish it with the one that we introduce in section 2.2. Here, we omit a rather extensive formulations of Transformer and refer readers to Vaswani et al. (2017), Radford et al. (2018) and Devlin et al. (2019) for more details.

## 2.2 HIDDEN REPRESENTATION EXTRACTOR

Transformer-based encoder normally has many structure-identical layers stacked together, for example, BERTLARGE and XLNetLARGE all contain 24 layers of the identical structure, either outputs

from these hidden layers or the last layer, but not only limited to the latter, may be extremely helpful for specific downstream task. To make full use of the representations from these hidden layers, we introduce an extra component attached to the original encoder, HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) to capture the complementary information that the output of the last layer fails to capture. Since each layer does not take the same importance to represent a certain input sequence for different downstream tasks, we design an adaptive mechanism that can compute the importance dynamically. We measure the importance by an **importance score** .

The input to the HIRE is _{_ _**H**_ 0 _, . . . ,_ _**H** j, . . . ,_ _**H** l}_ where _l_ represents the number of layers in the encoder. Here _**H**_ 0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training and _**H** j ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ is the hidden-state of the encoder at the output of layer _j_ . For the sake of simplicity, we call them all hidden-state afterwards.

We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (Cho et al., 2014) to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder. Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together. With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder’s final layer, HIRE’s output _**A**_ is expected to contain the additional useful information from the encoder’s hidden-states and we call it **complementary representation** .

## 2.3 FUSION NETWORK

This module fuses the information contained in the output of Transformer-based encoder and the one extracted from encoders’ hidden states by HIRE.

Given the preliminary representation _**R**_ , instead of letting it flow directly into task-specfic output layer, we combine it together with the complementary representation _**A**_ to yield _**M**_ , defined by:
where _◦_ is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard Product) and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.

Later, two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of _d_ for each direction, is used to fully fuse the information contained in the preliminary representation and the complementary representation. ## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 DATASET

We conducted the experiments on the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark (Wang et al., 2018) to evaluate our proposed method. GLUE is a collection of 9 diverse datasets[1] for training, evaluating, and analyzing natural language understanding models.

Table 1: GLUE Dev results. Our results are based on single model trained with single task and a median over five runs with different random seed but the same hyperparameter is reported for each task. The results of MT-DNN, XLNETLARGE, ALBERT and RoBERTa are from Liu et al. (2019b), Yang et al. (2019), Lan et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2019c). See the lower-most row for the performance of our approach. Mc, acc and pearson denote Matthews correlation, accuracy and Person correlation coefficient respectively.

Table 1 compares our method with a list of Transformer-based models on the development set. Model parameter comparison is shown in Table 2. To obtain a direct and fair comparison with our baseline model RoBERTa, following the original paper (Liu et al., 2019c), we fine-tune RoBERTa+HIRE separately for each of the GLUE tasks, using only task-specific training data. The single-model results for each task are reported. We run our model with five different random seeds but the same hyperparameters and take the median value. Due to the problematic nature of WNLI dataset, we exclude its results in this table. The results shows that RoBERTa+HIRE consistently outperforms RoBERTa on 4 of the GLUE task development sets, with an improvement of 1.7 points, 0.4 points, 0.5/0.2 points, 0.3 points on CoLA, SST-2, MNLI and QNLI respectively. And on the MRPC, STS-B and RTE task, our model get the same result as RoBERTa. It should be noted that the improvement is entirely attributed to the introduction of HIdden Representation Extractor and fusion network in our model.

Table 3: GLUE Test results, scored by the official evaluation server. All the results are obtained from GLUE leaderboard (https://gluebenchmark.com/leaderboard). (2019); _[b]_ Liu et al. (2019b); _[c]_ Zhu et al. (2020); _[d]_ Yang et al. (2019); _[e]_ Lan et al. (2020); _[f]_ Liu et al. (2019c).

Table 3 presents the results of HIRE enhancement and other models on the test set that have been submitted to the GLUE leaderboard. Following Liu et al. (2019c), we fine-tune STS-B and MRPC starting from the MNLI single-task model. Given the simplicity between RTE, WNLI and MNLI, and the large-scale nature of MNLI dataset (393k), we also initialize RoBERTa+HIRE with the weights of MNLI single-task model before fine-tuning on RTE and WNLI. We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard. The results show that RoBERTa+HIRE still boosts the strong RoBERTa baseline model on the test set. To be specific, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, SST-B, MNLI-mm and QNLI with an improvement of 0.8 points, 0.4 points, 0.7/0.9 points, 0.2/0.1 points, 0.2 points and 0.1 points respectively. In the meantime, RoBERTa+HIRE gets the same results as RoBERTa on QQP and WNLI. By category, RoBERTa+HIRE has better performance than RoBERTa on the single sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks. It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1. The results are quite promising since HIRE does not modify the encoder internal architecture (Yang et al., 2019) or redefine the pre-training procedure (Liu et al., 2019c) , getting the comparable results with them.

## 4 ABLATION STUDY

In this section, we perform a set of ablation experiments to understand the effects of our proposed techniques during fine-tuning. All the results reported in this section are a median of five random runs.

The results enable us to conclude that the hidden-state weighting mechanism introduced by HIRE may indeed adapt to diverse downstream tasks for better performance.

## 5 ANALYSIS

Figure 2: **Left** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers when computing the complementary representation for various NLU tasks. The numbers on the abscissa axis indicate the corresponding layer with 0 being the first layer and 24 being the last layer. **Right** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example.

We compare the importance score’s distribution of different NLU tasks. For each task, we run our best single model over the development set and the results are calculated by averaging the values across all the examples within each dataset. The results are presented in the left of Figure 2. From the top to the bottom of the heatmap, the results are placed in the following order: single-sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks, and natural language inference tasks. From it, we find that the distribution differs among the different tasks, which demonstrates HIRE’s dynamic ability to adapt for distinct tasks when computing the complimentary representation. The most important contribution occurs below the final layer for all the tasks except STS-B and RTE. All layers have a close contribution for STS-B and RTE task. In addition, we find that QNLI, a classification task which requires the model to determine whether the context sentence contains the answer to the question, relies on layers 2-4 to some extent. In fact, Jawahar et al. (2019) state that BERT mostly captures phrase-level (or span-level) information in the lower layers and that this information gets gradually diluted in higher layers. Since QNLI is derived from the span extraction question answering dataset

SQuAD, where the answer is a span of the input text, we speculate that HIRE takes advantage of this information when making the prediction.

The right of Figure 2 presents the distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example. It shows that HIRE can adapt not only distinct tasks but also the different examples. In the meantime, we observe also that even though there are subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain same patterns when calculating the complementary representation, for example, layers 21 and 22 contribute the most for almost all the examples and also the layers around them. But the figure shows also that for some examples, all layers contribute nearly equally.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Transformer model is empowered by self-attention mechanism and has been applied as an effective model architecture design in quite a lot of pre-trained language models (Vaswani et al., 2017). OpenAI GPT (Radford et al., 2018) is the first model that introduced Transformer architecture into unsupervised pre-training. But instead of unidirectional training like GPT, BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) adopts Masked LM objective when pre-training, which enables the representation to incorporate context from both direction. The next sentence prediction (NSP) objective is also used by BERT to better model the relationship between sentences. Trained with dynamic masking, large mini-batches and a larger byte-level BPE, full-sentences without NSP, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on BooksCorpus(Zhu et al., 2015), CC-News, Openwebtext and Stories. In terms of finetuning on downstream tasks, all these powerful Transformer-based models help various NLP tasks continuously achieve new state-of-the-art results. Diverse new methods have been proposed recently for fine-tuning the downstream tasks, including multi-task learning (Liu et al., 2019b), adversarial training (Zhu et al., 2020) or incorporating semantic information into language representation (Zhang et al., 2020).

Traditionally, downstream tasks or back-end part of models take representations from the last layer of the pre-trained language models as the default input. However, recent studies trigger researchers’ interest on the intermediate layers’ representation learned by pre-trained models. When studying the linguistic knowledge and transferability of contextualized word representations with a series of seventeen diverse probing tasks, Liu et al. (2019a) observe that Transformers tend to encode transferable features in their intermediate layers, aligned with the results by Peters et al. (2018b). Jawahar et al. (2019) show a rich hierarchy of linguistic information is encoded by BERT, with surface features, syntactic features and semantic features lying from bottom to top in the layers. So far, to our best knowledge, exploiting the representations learned by hidden layers is still stuck with limited empirical observations, which motivates us to propose a general solution for fully exploiting all levels of representations learned by the pre-trained language models. However, we want to note that a freezing/fine-tuning discrepancy may exist, since previous observations are made by adopting a feature extracting approach while our work is conducted in the fine-tuning scenario.

Several works have made attempts to utilize the information from the intermediate layers of deep models (Zhao et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018; Tenney et al., 2019). Peters et al. (2018a) propose a deep contextualized word representation called ELMo, which is a task-specific linear combination of the intermediate layer representations in the bidirectional LSTM language model. Tenney et al. (2019) find that using ELMo-style scalar mixing of layer activations, both deep Transformer models (BERT and GPT) gain a significant performance improvement on a novel edge probing task. Zhu et al. (2018) adopt a linear combination of embeddings from different layers in BERT to encode tokens for conversational question answering but in a feature-based way. Compared with these works, our approach still presents the following innovations: 1) Our dynamic layer weighting mechanism calculates importance scores for each example over different layers, which is distinct from previous works (e.g., ELMo), where the contribution score (in the form of a parameter) for each layer is always same for all the examples of the same task. 2) Our approach fuses the representation weighted from the hidden layers and the one from the last layer to get a refined representation. 3) To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.

## 7 CONCLUSION

This paper presents HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), a novel enhancement component that refines language representation by adaptively leveraging the Transformer-based model’s hidden layers. In our proposed model design, HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer. A lite fusion network then incorporates the outputs of HIRE into those of the original model. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of refined language representation for natural language understanding. The analysis highlights the distinct contribution of each layer’s output for diverse tasks and different examples.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper proposes HIRE (Hidden Representation Extractor), a module that aggregates hidden-layer outputs from pre-trained Transformer encoders (RoBERTa) via a shared Bi-GRU and learned importance weights, fused with the final layer's representation before the task head. While the motivation—that intermediate layers carry complementary information underutilized by standard fine-tuning—is reasonable and well-grounded in prior work (Peters et al., Devlin et al.), the contribution appears incremental, as layer-aggregation strategies (e.g., ELMo-style weighted scalar mixes, BERT feature-based NER combinations) are well established, and the paper does not sufficiently differentiate HIRE from these precedents either conceptually or empirically. The reported gains are also modest: outperforming the baseline on only 5/9 GLUE tasks and advancing SOTA on a single dataset (SST-2) is weak evidence for a method claiming to "deepen" pre-trained representations, especially without ablations isolating the Bi-GRU summarizer, the importance-weighting mechanism, and the two-step fusion, nor comparisons to simpler baselines like scalar-mixing or concatenation of selected layers. Clarity is adequate but uneven—the architecture description is reasonably precise, though the writing contains grammatical issues ("rival with," "onefold use"), the figure caption is terse, and key design choices (why concatenate Bi-GRU endpoint states rather than use pooled outputs; why a 2-layer GRU) are asserted without justification. Overall, the paper would need stronger empirical evidence, more rigorous ablations, and sharper positioning against existing layer-fusion techniques to meet the bar for a top venue.

comments.title: Inconsistency between claimed number of improved tasks and table
comments.quote: The results shows that RoBERTa+HIRE consistently outperforms RoBERTa on 4 of the GLUE task development sets, with an improvement of 1.7 points, 0.4 points, 0.5/0.2 points, 0.3 points on CoLA, SST-2, MNLI and QNLI respectively.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction claim HIRE 'outperforms the baseline on 5/9' GLUE tasks, but here the text says 4 tasks (CoLA, SST-2, MNLI, QNLI) with ties on MRPC, STS-B, RTE, and a small regression on QQP. Looking at Table 1, RoBERTa+HIRE actually matches RoBERTa on MRPC (90.9), STS-B (92.4), and RTE (86.6) and is slightly worse on QQP (92.0 vs 92.2). So the '5/9' claim in the abstract/intro and the '4' claim here are inconsistent with each other. Depending on how one counts MNLI-m/mm as one or two tasks, neither number is clearly supported. This is a real inconsistency that affects the main headline claim.

comments.title: Claim of SOTA on SST-2 is ambiguous given tie with other models
comments.quote: It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1.
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 3, RoBERTa+HIRE achieves 97.1 on SST-2, but XLNet and ALBERT are also shown with 97.1 (both bolded). So the model ties with — rather than surpasses — prior SOTA. Calling this 'state-of-the-art results' is defensible (it matches SOTA) but the phrasing 'advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2' in the abstract is a mild overclaim since the result only matches existing SOTA rather than advancing it.

comments.title: Self-fusion ablation does not cleanly isolate HIRE's contribution
comments.quote:
We remove the HIRE from our model and pass the preliminary representation _**R**_ directly into the fusion network. In order to keep the fusion network, we fuse the preliminary representation with itself, which means we define instead _**M**_ by:

_**M**_ = [ _**R**_ ; _**R**_ ; _**R**_ + _**R**_ ; _**R** ◦_ _**R**_ ] _∈_ R _[n][×]_[4] _[d]_
comments.explanation: This 'w/o HIRE' ablation substitutes A with R in the fusion operation, giving M = [R; R; 2R; R∘R]. Three of the four concatenated blocks are just linear functions of R (and the fourth is its Hadamard square), so the fusion network receives essentially redundant information. This doesn't really test 'fusion network without HIRE' — it tests 'fusion network with degenerate input.' The reported performance drop therefore cannot cleanly be attributed to the absence of HIRE's complementary information; it could equally reflect the degeneracy of the input to the fusion network. A cleaner ablation would feed only R through a GRU of matching capacity. This limits the interpretability of the ablation's conclusion.

comments.title: Ensemble vs. single-model comparison in Table 3 is not clearly disclosed
comments.quote: We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard.
comments.explanation: The authors state their Table 3 submissions are ensemble results. However, the models they are comparing against (XLNet, ALBERT, RoBERTa, etc.) on the GLUE leaderboard are also typically ensembles, so this is probably fine in context. My concern is that the text doesn't specify whether the baseline RoBERTa row in Table 3 represents single-model or ensemble results, making the point-wise comparisons (+0.8, +0.4, etc.) somewhat ambiguous. A careful reader comparing HIRE's ensemble against RoBERTa's single model would draw incorrect conclusions about the benefit of HIRE. This should be clarified for a fair comparison claim.

comments.title: Overstated novelty claim about fine-tuning with intermediate layers
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model's intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: This is a strong claim of priority. The authors distinguish their work from feature-based approaches (ELMo, Zhu et al. 2018) and probing studies, which is reasonable. However, by 2020 there were already published works exploiting intermediate BERT/Transformer layer representations during fine-tuning (e.g., Kondratyuk & Straka's UDify uses layer attention over all BERT layers during fine-tuning; Su & Cheng's SesameBERT; several papers on layer aggregation for fine-tuning). The claim 'first one' is therefore an overclaim. Even granting a narrower interpretation, the authors do not precisely define what they mean by 'takes advantage of... in the context of fine-tuning' in a way that excludes these prior works. This is a mild overclaim that weakens the related work positioning but does not undermine the empirical contribution.

comments.title: Underspecified 'random' baseline for importance scores
comments.quote: On the contrary, under the _random_ condition, we generate a score for each layer randomly and do the SoftMax operation across all the layers.
comments.explanation: The random baseline is described very briefly. Key details are missing: Is the random score sampled once (fixed for training/inference) or re-sampled per example/batch? What distribution is used? Is it fixed across the five random runs or resampled each run? These choices materially affect what the ablation tests. Without this, a reader cannot reproduce the comparison nor interpret whether the 'random' is a fair stand-in for a non-adaptive but non-uniform baseline. This is a moderate reproducibility concern for an ablation that the paper uses to argue for the 'dynamic' mechanism.

comments.title: Claim about importance-score interpretability may overreach
comments.quote: Since QNLI is derived from the span extraction question answering dataset SQuAD, where the answer is a span of the input text, we speculate that HIRE takes advantage of this information when making the prediction.
comments.explanation: The authors appropriately hedge with 'we speculate', so this is soft. However, the preceding sentences frame the QNLI layer-2–4 weighting as evidence aligned with Jawahar et al. (2019)'s claim about phrase/span information in lower BERT layers. Two concerns: (1) QNLI as reformulated for GLUE is a sentence-pair classification task (does the sentence contain the answer), not span extraction, so the connection to span-level info is indirect; (2) importance scores in an attention-weighted sum over layer summaries are notoriously hard to interpret causally — high weight does not necessarily mean the layer provides the useful signal for the task, especially when the summary is compressed via Bi-GRU to 4d. The hedge ('speculate') keeps this from being a hard error, but readers should be aware the interpretability argument is loose.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a reasonable and well-motivated question—whether downstream fine-tuning should rely only on the final layer of a pretrained Transformer—but the contribution appears incremental and the presentation needs substantial tightening. The core idea of learning task-adaptive combinations of intermediate representations is related to prior work such as ELMo-style layer weighting, BERT layer mixing, scalar mix, and probing analyses, so the paper must more clearly distinguish HIRE from existing layer-fusion mechanisms and justify why a relatively heavy BiGRU-based extractor is preferable to simpler learned weighted sums or attention over layers. The writing is understandable at a high level but contains frequent grammatical issues, awkward phrasing, inconsistent terminology, and formatting problems that reduce clarity; the model description in particular is underspecified, with missing equations, unclear tensor shapes, and insufficient explanation of how HIRE outputs are fused back with token-level representations. The motivation that the final layer is insufficient is plausible, but the empirical claims in the introduction seem modest—improvements on only 5/9 GLUE tasks and one state-of-the-art result—so strong ablations, variance over random seeds, parameter/control comparisons, and statistical significance are essential to show that gains are due to the proposed architecture rather than added parameters or tuning. The most significant issues are therefore novelty relative to existing layer aggregation methods, lack of rigorous justification for the design choices, and insufficient clarity in the technical exposition; strengthening the related-work positioning, simplifying and formalizing the method description, and providing careful ablations would substantially improve the paper.

comments.title: Prior-work and novelty claims about intermediate-layer use are too broad
comments.quote: However, current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder’s final layer when fine-tuning the downstream tasks. / To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The paper’s intended contrast with the standard BERT/RoBERTa fine-tuning recipe is understandable: many common setups feed only the final-layer [CLS]/<s> or final token representations into a task head. However, the broader claims that current approaches only use final-layer outputs and that this work is the first to exploit intermediate PLM information during fine-tuning are overstated. By the time of the paper, layer mixing, concatenation, layer attention, feature-extraction variants, and related intermediate-representation methods were already known around ELMo/BERT-style models, and the authors themselves discuss prior evidence that different BERT layer combinations had been explored. The contribution may still be novel in its particular example-dependent weighting and fusion architecture, but the novelty framing should be more carefully qualified.

comments.title: Claim of advancing SST-2 state of the art appears to be a tie, not an advance
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that their SST-2 test score is very strong and improves over the RoBERTa baseline reported later in the paper. However, Table 3 reports RoBERTa+HIRE at 97.1 on SST-2, while XLNet and ALBERT are also reported at 97.1. On the dev table, RoBERTa+HIRE is 96.8, below XLNet’s 97.0. Thus the result appears to match the best listed test performance rather than surpass it. If the intended meaning is “achieves state-of-the-art performance,” that would be defensible; “advances” suggests a new higher score and is not supported by the reported comparisons.

comments.title: HIRE’s “complementary” representation may include the final-layer representation it is said to complement
comments.quote: HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture. / HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer.
comments.explanation: The intuition that intermediate layers may contain useful information absent or attenuated in the final Transformer layer is reasonable. However, the formal construction does not enforce that interpretation. HIRE takes inputs from \(\mathbf{H}_0\) through \(\mathbf{H}_l\), where \(\mathbf{H}_l\) is the encoder’s final-layer output, and forms \(\mathbf{A}=\sum_i s_i\mathbf{H}_i\). Therefore \(\mathbf{A}\) can include, and may heavily weight, the same final-layer representation \(\mathbf{R}\) that it is described as complementing. Conversely, the conclusion’s wording appears to say HIRE uses hidden layers “other than” the default last layer, which contradicts the equations if read literally. Unless the final layer is excluded or an explicit complementarity/orthogonality constraint is added, \(\mathbf{A}\) is more accurately described as an adaptive layer-aggregated auxiliary representation.

comments.title: GRU final-state summarization leaves padding/masking behavior underspecified
comments.quote: Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together.
comments.explanation: The high-level design is coherent: each Transformer hidden-state sequence is summarized by a shared 2-layer Bi-GRU, and concatenating final states from two layers and two directions explains the resulting 4d vector. What remains unclear is how “final state” is defined for variable-length, padded batches. In Transformer fine-tuning, sequences are commonly padded, and a vanilla GRU run over padded tensors will produce forward final states at the padded end unless sequence lengths are packed or masks are otherwise used. Since these final states determine the layer importance scores, the omission creates a localized reproducibility gap.

comments.title: Fusion-network GRU output dimensionality is ambiguous
comments.quote: Later, two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of _d_ for each direction, is used to fully fuse the information contained in the preliminary representation and the complementary representation. We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation:
comments.explanation: The later classifier dimensions suggest the intended token-level fusion output has dimension 2d, i.e. the top BiGRU layer’s forward and backward outputs are concatenated. However, the phrase “concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together” is imprecise. Because the GRU is two-layer and bidirectional, a reader could wonder whether outputs across both layers and both directions are concatenated, which would yield 4d. Earlier, HIRE explicitly concatenates each layer and direction’s final states, so the distinction matters. The method description should clarify whether only the final recurrent layer’s bidirectional outputs are used in the fusion network.

comments.title: Performance comparisons lack enough controlled protocol detail to attribute gains specifically to HIRE
comments.quote: It should be noted that the improvement is entirely attributed to the introduction of HIdden Representation Extractor and fusion network in our model. / Table 3: GLUE Test results, scored by the official evaluation server. All the results are obtained from GLUE leaderboard (https://gluebenchmark.com/leaderboard).
comments.explanation: The reported results are consistent with HIRE and the fusion network helping, and the later ablations provide some support for that interpretation. However, the attribution language is too strong. In Table 1, the RoBERTa baseline numbers are taken from Liu et al. rather than clearly being re-run under the same codebase, hyperparameter search, random seeds, and reporting convention as RoBERTa+HIRE. Table 3 further compares leaderboard submissions that may differ in ensembling, model selection, task-specific tricks, transfer initialization, and number of submissions; the surrounding text indicates RoBERTa+HIRE is an ensemble submission, while the table does not make the single-model versus ensemble status of rows clear. These protocol differences make it difficult to isolate the effect of HIRE, especially when several gains are small.

comments.title: RoBERTa pretraining corpus description omits English Wikipedia
comments.quote: Trained with dynamic masking, large mini-batches and a larger byte-level BPE, full-sentences without NSP, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on BooksCorpus(Zhu et al., 2015), CC-News, Openwebtext and Stories.
comments.explanation: The high-level characterization of RoBERTa as a stronger BERT reimplementation using dynamic masking, larger batches, no NSP, and more data is correct. However, the standard RoBERTa pretraining corpus includes BookCorpus, English Wikipedia, CC-News, OpenWebText, and Stories. This passage lists BookCorpus, CC-News, OpenWebText, and Stories but omits English Wikipedia. This does not affect the proposed HIRE method, but it is a factual inaccuracy in the background.

comments.title: “Fully exploiting all levels” overstates what the proposed layer weighting demonstrates
comments.quote: So far, to our best knowledge, exploiting the representations learned by hidden layers is still stuck with limited empirical observations, which motivates us to propose a general solution for fully exploiting all levels of representations learned by the pre-trained language models.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intent is clear: they want to move beyond observing that intermediate layers contain useful information, and their method does use all encoder states through learned weights. Still, “fully exploiting all levels” is stronger than what the method or experiments establish. HIRE compresses each layer to a sequence-level summary, assigns one scalar importance weight per layer for the whole example, and forms a single weighted sum. That is a useful aggregation mechanism, but it does not show that all layer information is fully used, nor that token-specific, task-specific, or interaction-level information across layers is exhausted. A softer phrase such as “adaptively combining” or “systematically leveraging” all levels would be more justified.

comments.title: RTE reference appears to reverse authors’ given and family names
comments.quote: - BarHaim Roy, Dagan Ido, Dolan Bill, Ferro Lisa, Giampiccolo Danilo, Magnini Bernardo, and Szpektor Idan. The second PASCAL recognising textual entailment challenge. In _In Proceedings of the Second PASCAL Challenges Workshop on Recognising Textual Entailment_ , 2006.
comments.explanation: The surrounding references use conventional given-name–family-name order, but this RTE-2 citation appears to reverse each author’s names. The paper is normally attributed to Roy Bar-Haim, Ido Dagan, Bill Dolan, Lisa Ferro, Danilo Giampiccolo, Bernardo Magnini, and Idan Szpektor. The citation also writes “BarHaim” without the usual hyphen. This is a minor bibliographic error rather than a methodological issue.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a reasonable and well-motivated question—whether downstream fine-tuning should rely only on the final layer of a pretrained Transformer—but the contribution appears incremental and the presentation needs substantial tightening. The core idea of learning task-adaptive combinations of intermediate representations is related to prior work such as ELMo-style layer weighting, BERT layer mixing, scalar mix, and probing analyses, so the paper must more clearly distinguish HIRE from existing layer-fusion mechanisms and justify why a relatively heavy BiGRU-based extractor is preferable to simpler learned weighted sums or attention over layers. The writing is understandable at a high level but contains frequent grammatical issues, awkward phrasing, inconsistent terminology, and formatting problems that reduce clarity; the model description in particular is underspecified, with missing equations, unclear tensor shapes, and insufficient explanation of how HIRE outputs are fused back with token-level representations. The motivation that the final layer is insufficient is plausible, but the empirical claims in the introduction seem modest—improvements on only 5/9 GLUE tasks and one state-of-the-art result—so strong ablations, variance over random seeds, parameter/control comparisons, and statistical significance are essential to show that gains are due to the proposed architecture rather than added parameters or tuning. The most significant issues are therefore novelty relative to existing layer aggregation methods, lack of rigorous justification for the design choices, and insufficient clarity in the technical exposition; strengthening the related-work positioning, simplifying and formalizing the method description, and providing careful ablations would substantially improve the paper.

comments.title: Overbroad claim that current fine-tuning approaches use only the final layer
comments.quote: However, current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder’s final layer when fine-tuning the downstream tasks.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: the standard BERT/RoBERTa fine-tuning recipe commonly feeds the final-layer [CLS]/<s> representation, or final token representations, into a task head. In that narrower sense, the motivation is reasonable and is consistent with the later description of a baseline RoBERTa setup. However, the statement as written is broader than that: by 2021 there were already well-known approaches that used learned scalar mixtures or concatenations of multiple layers, including ELMo-style layer mixing and BERT feature-extraction variants, and the authors themselves cite Devlin et al. as evidence that different BERT layer combinations had been tried. The issue is not with the method, but with the framing: “current approaches only” overstates the gap and could mislead readers about prior work. A qualification such as “standard fine-tuning approaches often” would be more accurate.

comments.title: Claim of advancing SST-2 state of the art appears to be a tie, not an advance
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that their SST-2 test score is very strong and improves over the RoBERTa baseline reported later in the paper. I checked the full results table to see whether “advances” is supported. Table 3 reports RoBERTa+HIRE at 97.1 on SST-2, but XLNet and ALBERT are also reported at 97.1. On the dev table, RoBERTa+HIRE is 96.8, below XLNet’s 97.0. Thus the result appears to match the best listed test performance rather than surpass it. If the intended meaning is “achieves state-of-the-art performance,” that would be defensible; “advances” suggests a new higher score and is not supported by the reported comparisons.

comments.title: “Complementary” representation is not formally constrained to exclude the final-layer representation
comments.quote: HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture.
comments.explanation: The intended intuition is clear: HIRE should retrieve useful information from intermediate representations that may be absent or attenuated in the final Transformer layer. The formal construction, however, does not quite match the stronger wording. In Section 2.2, HIRE takes inputs from _**H**_0 through _**H**_l, where _**H**_l is the output of the final encoder layer, and the complementary representation _**A**_ is later formed as a weighted combination over these states. Therefore _**A**_ can include, and in some cases may heavily weight, the same final-layer representation _**R**_ that it is described as complementing. This does not make the method invalid—the learned mixture may still provide useful auxiliary information—but it weakens the interpretation that HIRE specifically captures only information “that the final layer’s output fails to capture.” A more precise description would call _**A**_ an adaptive layer-aggregated or auxiliary representation, unless the final layer is excluded or an explicit orthogonality/complementarity constraint is added.

comments.title: GRU final-state summarization leaves padding/masking behavior underspecified
comments.quote: Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together.
comments.explanation: The paper gives a coherent high-level design: each Transformer hidden-state sequence is summarized by a shared 2-layer Bi-GRU, and concatenating final states from two layers and two directions explains the resulting 4d vector. What remains unclear is how “final state” is defined for variable-length, padded batches. In Transformer fine-tuning, sequences are commonly padded, and a vanilla GRU run over padded tensors will produce forward final states at the padded end unless lengths are packed or masks are otherwise used. This matters here because these final states determine the layer importance scores. A reader can guess that standard sequence lengths or attention masks are used, but the procedure is not stated, and different implementations could yield different HIRE scores. This is a localized reproducibility gap rather than a conceptual flaw.

comments.title: Fusion-network GRU output dimensionality is ambiguous
comments.quote: Later, two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of _d_ for each direction, is used to fully fuse the information contained in the preliminary representation and the complementary representation. We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation:
comments.explanation: I first read this as a fairly standard use of a stacked BiGRU: with hidden size d in each direction, the token-level output of the top recurrent layer would have dimension 2d. That interpretation is supported later, where the classification vector is stated to have dimension 2d and the first FFN matrix has shape 2d by d. However, the phrase “concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together” is not precise. Because the GRU is described as two-layer and bidirectional, a reader could reasonably wonder whether the authors concatenate outputs across both layers and both directions, which would give 4d rather than 2d. Earlier in HIRE, the paper explicitly concatenates each layer and direction’s final states, so the distinction matters. The later output-layer dimensions mostly resolve the intended shape, but the fusion-network description should clarify whether only the final BiGRU layer’s forward/backward outputs are used, or whether all recurrent layers contribute.

comments.title: “Complementary representation” is stronger than what the HIRE formula guarantees
comments.quote: With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder’s final layer, HIRE’s output _**A**_ is expected to contain the additional useful information from the encoder’s hidden-states and we call it **complementary representation** .
comments.explanation: The intended motivation is clear: a learned weighted combination of hidden states may expose useful information not present in the final layer alone. Given the preceding mechanism, this is a reasonable design hypothesis. What remains problematic is that the construction itself does not enforce complementarity. The weights are a softmax over layers, and the weighted sum apparently includes the final layer as well as earlier hidden states; there is no residualization against R, orthogonality constraint, information bottleneck, or objective term that would make A contain only information missing from the final representation. Thus A is better described as an adaptively aggregated hidden-layer representation. Calling it “complementary” is acceptable as informal terminology, but the prose slightly overstates what follows from the formal definition.

comments.title: Causal attribution of dev-set gains is stronger than the comparison supports
comments.quote: It should be noted that the improvement is entirely attributed to the introduction of HIdden Representation Extractor and fusion network in our model.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that the architectural difference between RoBERTa and RoBERTa+HIRE is the added HIRE/fusion machinery, and the later ablations help support the usefulness of those components. However, in Table 1 the RoBERTa baseline numbers are taken from Liu et al. (2019c), whereas RoBERTa+HIRE is reported as the median over the authors’ five runs. The comparison also introduces a substantial number of extra parameters, as Table 2 shows. Because the baseline is not clearly re-run under the same codebase, hyperparameter search, random seeds, and reporting convention, and because some gains are small, the word “entirely” is too strong. The results are consistent with HIRE/fusion helping, but they do not by themselves rule out effects from implementation details, tuning, stochastic variation, or added capacity.

comments.title: Leaderboard test comparison lacks enough protocol detail to isolate the effect of HIRE
comments.quote: Table 3: GLUE Test results, scored by the official evaluation server. All the results are obtained from GLUE leaderboard (https://gluebenchmark.com/leaderboard).
comments.explanation: Using the official GLUE server is appropriate for test-set reporting, and citing leaderboard entries is standard. The concern is comparability and reproducibility rather than the numerical table itself. Leaderboard submissions can differ in ensembling, transfer initialization, task-specific tricks, number of submissions, and model-selection procedure. In the surrounding context, RoBERTa+HIRE is described as an ensemble-model submission, while Table 3 labels the row simply as “RoBERTa+HIRE” and the competing rows are cited from separate leaderboard submissions. Without a clear statement in or near the table of which rows are single models versus ensembles and the exact HIRE ensembling protocol, the table is less suitable for attributing the reported test improvements specifically to HIRE. This is especially relevant because several reported differences are only 0.1–0.4 points.

comments.title: Conclusion appears to say HIRE excludes the final layer, contradicting the model definition
comments.quote: HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer.
comments.explanation: My first reading was that this sentence is using “other than” informally to mean “rather than relying only on” the last layer. However, in standard usage, “other than that from the default last layer” reads as excluding the final-layer representation from HIRE’s weighted combination. That is not what the formal model earlier defines: HIRE takes \(\{\mathbf{H}_0,\ldots,\mathbf{H}_l\}\), where \(\mathbf{H}_l\) is the encoder’s final-layer output, and computes \(\mathbf{A}=\sum_{i=0}^{l}s_i\mathbf{H}_i\). Thus the final layer is included in the HIRE mixture and is then also separately fused as \(\mathbf{R}\). If the authors intend exclusion, the equations and experiments would differ; if they intend inclusion, this sentence should say something like “from all encoder states, in addition to the default last-layer representation.”

comments.title: Novelty claim about being first to use intermediate PLM information during fine-tuning is too broad
comments.quote: 3) To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The authors do make a reasonable distinction between several cited prior works: ELMo uses task-specific scalar mixing but is not a Transformer fine-tuning setup; Tenney et al. use scalar mixing mainly for probing; and Zhu et al. are described as feature-based. That context partially supports their intended novelty claim. What remains problematic is the breadth of “the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.” Intermediate-layer concatenation, scalar mixing, layer attention, auxiliary/deep supervision, and related layer aggregation ideas were already common enough around BERT-style models that such a firstness claim needs a much more careful qualification. The paper’s contribution can still be novel—especially the example-dependent layer weighting plus fusion network—but the current wording overclaims relative to the evidence provided in the related-work discussion.

comments.title: RoBERTa pretraining corpus description omits English Wikipedia
comments.quote: Trained with dynamic masking, large mini-batches and a larger byte-level BPE, full-sentences without NSP, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on BooksCorpus(Zhu et al., 2015), CC-News, Openwebtext and Stories.
comments.explanation: The high-level point—that RoBERTa is a stronger BERT reimplementation using dynamic masking, larger batches, no NSP, and more data—is correct. However, checking against the standard RoBERTa description, the pretraining corpus includes BookCorpus, English Wikipedia, CC-News, OpenWebText, and Stories. This passage lists BooksCorpus, CC-News, OpenWebText, and Stories but omits English Wikipedia. This does not affect the proposed HIRE method, but it is a factual inaccuracy in the background that could mislead readers about what data RoBERTa used.

comments.title: “Fully exploiting all levels” overstates what the proposed layer weighting actually demonstrates
comments.quote: So far, to our best knowledge, exploiting the representations learned by hidden layers is still stuck with limited empirical observations, which motivates us to propose a general solution for fully exploiting all levels of representations learned by the pre-trained language models.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: they want to move beyond merely observing that intermediate layers contain useful information, and their method does use all encoder states \(\mathbf{H}_0,\ldots,\mathbf{H}_l\) through learned weights. Still, “fully exploiting all levels” is stronger than what the method or experiments establish. HIRE compresses each layer to a sequence-level summary, assigns one scalar importance weight per layer for the whole example, and forms a single weighted sum. That is a useful aggregation mechanism, but it does not show that all layer information is fully used, nor that token-specific, task-specific, or interaction-level information across layers is exhausted. A softer formulation such as “systematically leveraging” or “adaptively combining” all levels would be more justified.

comments.title: RTE reference appears to reverse authors’ given and family names
comments.quote: - BarHaim Roy, Dagan Ido, Dolan Bill, Ferro Lisa, Giampiccolo Danilo, Magnini Bernardo, and Szpektor Idan. The second PASCAL recognising textual entailment challenge. In _In Proceedings of the Second PASCAL Challenges Workshop on Recognising Textual Entailment_ , 2006.
comments.explanation: I first considered whether this was merely a bibliography style that prints family names before given names, but the surrounding references consistently use the conventional given-name–family-name order, e.g. “Zhenzhong Lan,” “Nelson F. Liu,” and “Richard Socher.” The cited RTE-2 paper is normally attributed to Roy Bar-Haim, Ido Dagan, Bill Dolan, Lisa Ferro, Danilo Giampiccolo, Bernardo Magnini, and Idan Szpektor. Here each name appears reversed, and “BarHaim” also appears without the usual hyphen. This does not affect the paper’s method or results, and a knowledgeable reader could probably identify the intended reference, but it is a real bibliographic error rather than an OCR-level symbol issue.
